mission.title: Install and Configure Kagenti on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kagenti is a Kubernetes-based control plane for AI agents. It provides a framework-neutral, scalable, and secure platform for deploying and orchestrating AI agents through standardized agent communication protocols (A2A, MCP). Kagenti works with any agent framework (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, etc.) and offers modular components that can be deployed independently or as a complete platform.

steps.title: Verify prerequisites
steps.description:
Ensure you have the required tools installed before proceeding:
- **Python >= 3.9** with the `uv` package manager
- **Docker Desktop**, Rancher Desktop, or Podman (16 GB RAM, 4 cores minimum)
- **Kind** for local Kubernetes clusters
- **kubectl** for cluster interaction
- **Helm** for chart management
- **Ollama** (optional) for local LLM inference

Verify your setup:
```bash
python3 --version
docker info
kind version
kubectl version --client
helm version
```

steps.title: Clone the Kagenti repository
steps.description:
Clone the Kagenti repository and navigate into the project directory:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/kagenti/kagenti.git
cd kagenti
```

steps.title: Configure secrets
steps.description:
Copy the example secrets file and configure it with your LLM provider API keys and other settings:
```bash
cp deployments/envs/secret_values.yaml.example deployments/envs/.secret_values.yaml
```
Edit `deployments/envs/.secret_values.yaml` with your API keys for OpenAI, Anthropic, or other LLM providers you plan to use.

steps.title: Run the installer
steps.description:
Use the Ansible-based installer to deploy Kagenti to a local Kind cluster:
```bash
deployments/ansible/run-install.sh --env dev
```
For OpenShift deployments, consult the Kagenti documentation for environment-specific flags. Use `--help` for additional options:
```bash
deployments/ansible/run-install.sh --help
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all Kagenti components are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -A | grep kagenti
kubectl get pods -n kagenti-system
```
You should see pods for the Kagenti UI, MCP Gateway, Agent Lifecycle Operator, and other core components.

steps.title: Access the Kagenti dashboard
steps.description:
Open the Kagenti UI to manage agents, tools, and observe traffic:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/kagenti-ui -n kagenti-system 8082:80
```
Then open http://localhost:8082 in your browser.

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show all Kagenti pods running in the kagenti-system namespace. The Kagenti UI will be accessible via port-forward on port 8082, where you can deploy agents, configure MCP tools, and monitor agent-to-agent communication.

uninstall.title: Run the Kagenti uninstaller
uninstall.description:
Use the Ansible-based uninstaller to remove all Kagenti components:
```bash
deployments/ansible/run-install.sh --env dev --uninstall
```
If the uninstaller is not available, remove the Helm releases manually:
```bash
helm list -A | grep kagenti | awk '{print $1, $2}' | xargs -n2 helm uninstall -n
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and namespace
uninstall.description:
Remove Kagenti Custom Resource Definitions and the namespace:
```bash
kubectl delete crd --selector=app.kubernetes.io/part-of=kagenti
kubectl delete namespace kagenti-system
```

uninstall.title: Delete the Kind cluster (optional)
uninstall.description:
If you created a dedicated Kind cluster for Kagenti:
```bash
kind delete cluster --name kagenti
```

upgrade.title: Pull latest Kagenti code
upgrade.description:
Update your local clone to the latest version:
```bash
cd kagenti
git pull origin main
```

upgrade.title: Re-run the installer
upgrade.description:
The installer is idempotent and will upgrade existing components:
```bash
deployments/ansible/run-install.sh --env dev
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that all pods are running the updated versions:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kagenti-system
kubectl describe deployment kagenti-ui -n kagenti-system | grep Image
```

troubleshooting.title: Installer fails with Docker errors
troubleshooting.description:
Ensure Docker Desktop (or Podman) is running and has at least 16 GB RAM and 4 CPU cores allocated. Check with:
```bash
docker info | grep -E 'Memory|CPUs'
```

troubleshooting.title: Kind cluster creation fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kind cluster fails to create, delete any existing cluster and retry:
```bash
kind delete cluster --name kagenti
deployments/ansible/run-install.sh --env dev
```

troubleshooting.title: MCP Gateway not routing requests
troubleshooting.description:
Verify the MCP Gateway pod is running and check its logs:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kagenti-system -l app=mcp-gateway
kubectl logs -n kagenti-system -l app=mcp-gateway --tail=50
```

troubleshooting.title: Agent pods stuck in Pending
troubleshooting.description:
Check for resource constraints on the cluster nodes:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n kagenti-system
kubectl top nodes
```
Consider increasing Docker Desktop resource allocation.

troubleshooting.title: LLM API key errors
troubleshooting.description:
Verify your API keys are correctly configured in the secrets file:
```bash
cat deployments/envs/.secret_values.yaml
```
Ensure the keys are valid and have sufficient quota with your LLM provider.

prerequisites.description: Requires Python >= 3.9 with uv, Docker Desktop (16 GB RAM, 4 cores), Kind, kubectl, and Helm. Ollama is optional for local LLM inference.
